sentences.base_language: hello, my name is seth.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Me llamo** = I call myself — from the reflexive verb **llamarse**. More natural than **mi nombre es** in everyday speech.
Subject pronoun **yo** is dropped — the **-o** ending already signals first person singular.

sentences.base_language: What is your name?
sentences.grammarNote:
**Llamarse** is a reflexive verb meaning 'to be called'. Literally: 'How do you call yourself?'
**te llamas** = 2nd person singular informal. Formal: **¿Cómo se llama usted?**

sentences.base_language: I am from the united states.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Soy de** = I'm from — origin uses **ser** (permanent trait).
**los estados unidos** = the United States — note **los** (masculine plural article). In Spanish, 'the' is used before country names in this phrase.

sentences.base_language: but I live in Uruguay.
sentences.grammarNote:
**vivo** — **vivir** is a regular -ir verb: **vivo · vives · vive · vivimos · viven**.
**en** = in (for countries, cities): **en Uruguay** · **en México** · **en Madrid**.
Note **pero** (but) — introduces a contrasting fact after the previous sentence.

sentences.base_language: I'm trying to learn spanish.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Estoy tratando de** (I am trying to) uses **estar + gerund** for an ongoing action, followed by **de + infinitive**.
**estar + gerund** is the Spanish progressive: **estoy comiendo** (I'm eating) · **estoy aprendiendo** (I'm learning).

sentences.base_language: Do you want to learn with me?
sentences.grammarNote:
**quieres** — **querer** stem-changes e→ie: **quiero · quieres · quiere**.
**conmigo** = with me — the fused form of **con + mí** (never *con mi* or *con mí*).

sentences.base_language: My girlfriend is named Kayla.
sentences.grammarNote:
**novia** = girlfriend (also fiancée or bride depending on context). **novio** = boyfriend.
**se llama** = she is called/named — third person reflexive of **llamarse**.

sentences.base_language: She is a nice woman.
sentences.grammarNote:
**simpática** = nice/friendly (fem.) — a false friend! It does NOT mean 'sympathetic' in English.
**ser** is used for permanent personality traits: **es simpática** (she's a nice person).
Adjective **simpática** follows the noun and agrees in gender.

sentences.base_language: We like to go on walks.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Nos gusta** = we like (literally: it pleases us) — **nos** is the first-person plural indirect object.
**salir a caminar** = to go out walking — **salir a + infinitive** means to go out to do something.

sentences.base_language: We like to cook together.
sentences.grammarNote:
**cocinar** = to cook (regular -ar verb).
**juntos** = together (masculine/mixed plural) — agrees with the implied **nosotros** subject.
If both were women: **juntas** (feminine plural).

sentences.base_language: She is a really good chef.
sentences.target_language: Ella es una muy buena cocinera.
sentences.grammarNote:
**cocinera** = female cook/chef. **cocinero** = male. The -o/-a ending marks gender for many profession nouns.
**ser** for profession — a defining characteristic: **es cocinera** (she's a cook/chef).
**buena** is placed before the noun here for emphasis/affection.

sentences.base_language: I am not a great chef but I have a secret trick.
sentences.target_language: No soy un gran cocinero pero tengo un truco secreto.
sentences.grammarNote:
**gran** = the shortened form of **grande** before any noun (masculine or feminine): **gran cocinero** · **gran idea**.
**gran** before a noun often implies 'great' in quality, not just size.
Word order: Spanish adjectives usually follow the noun: **truco secreto** (secret trick).

sentences.base_language: I use a lot of Siracha sauce!
sentences.grammarNote:
**mucha** — **mucho** agrees with the noun: **mucho** (masc.) / **mucha** (fem.) / **muchos** / **muchas**.
**salsa** = sauce (feminine) → **mucha salsa**.
**uso** — **usar** is a regular -ar verb: I use.

sentences.base_language: My best friend is Jeff.
sentences.grammarNote:
**mejor** = better/best — **bueno** has an irregular comparative: **bueno → mejor** (not *más bueno*).
**mi mejor amigo** = my best friend — **mejor** is invariable (doesn't change for gender): **mi mejor amigo** · **mi mejor amiga**.

sentences.base_language: Yes, the jeff that made this app.
sentences.target_language: Sí, el Jeff que hizo esta aplicación.
sentences.grammarNote:
**que** = who/that — a relative pronoun used for both people and things in Spanish.
**hizo** — preterite of **hacer**: he/she/it made. Highly irregular: **hice · hiciste · hizo · hicimos · hicieron**.
**esta aplicación** — **esta** (feminine) agrees with **aplicación** (feminine).

sentences.base_language: He works as a programmer.
sentences.grammarNote:
**como** = as/like (no accent) — used for comparisons and roles.
**programador** — no article with professions after **como**: **trabaja como programador** (not *como un programador*).
**Él** (he) is included here for emphasis or clarity — normally dropped.

sentences.base_language: I have many other friends too.
sentences.grammarNote:
**también** = also/too — placed at the start here for emphasis (it can go in various positions).
**muchos otros amigos** = many other friends — both **muchos** and **otros** are masculine plural, agreeing with **amigos**.

sentences.base_language: Stephen and Leslie are very nice.
sentences.target_language: Stephen y Leslie son muy simpáticos.
sentences.grammarNote:
**simpáticos** — masculine plural because the group includes at least one male (Stephen). Even a mixed group defaults to masculine plural.
**son** — **ser** for permanent personality traits.
**y** (and) stays **y** here — it only changes to **e** before words starting with i/hi.

sentences.base_language: They do a lot of hobbies.
sentences.grammarNote:
**pasatiempos** = hobbies — literally 'time-passers' (**pasar** + **tiempo**).
**Tienen** — **tener** is irregular in yo (**tengo**) but regular in third person plural: **tienen**.
Subject pronoun **ellos** is dropped — **tienen** makes it clear.

sentences.base_language: Leslie can make really pretty costumes.
sentences.target_language: Leslie puede hacer disfraces muy bonitos.
sentences.grammarNote:
**puede hacer** — **poder + infinitive**: can do/make.
**disfraces** = costumes — singular: **disfraz** (ends in -z, plural changes z→c: **disfraces**).
**bonitos** = pretty/nice (masculine plural, agreeing with **disfraces**).

sentences.base_language: Stephen likes music festivals.
sentences.target_language: A Stephen le gustan los festivales de música.
sentences.grammarNote:
When the person who likes something is a name (not a pronoun), Spanish adds **a** before it: **A Stephen le gustan...**
**le gustan** — plural because **los festivales** (the festivals) is the grammatical subject.
Compare: **Le gusta la música** (singular) vs **Le gustan los festivales** (plural).

sentences.base_language: Together they are a lot of fun.
sentences.grammarNote:
**divertidos** = fun/entertaining — masculine plural, agreeing with the implied **ellos** (Stephen and Leslie).
**ser** for characteristic personality/quality: **son divertidos** (they are fun people — a defining trait).
**Juntos** (together) is placed first for emphasis.

sentences.base_language: What are your friends like? Do you have a boyfriend or girlfriend?
sentences.target_language: ¿Cómo son tus amigos? ¿Tienes novio o novia?
sentences.grammarNote:
**¿Cómo son?** = What are they like? — **cómo** with **ser** asks about characteristics/descriptions.
**tus amigos** — **tu** (your) becomes **tus** before plural nouns.
**novio o novia** = boyfriend or girlfriend — both listed to include all possibilities.
